title: Entanglement of multi-qubit quantum graph states and studies structural properties of tripartite graphs with quantum programming
abstract: We propose a method for constructing multi-qubit entangled quantum states representing weighted tripartite graphs. An expression for the entanglement distance for multi-qubit states corresponding to arbitrary tripartite graph structures is obtained. The entanglement of a qubit with the rest of the system in a quantum graph state is determined by the weights of the edges in the closed neighborhood of the corresponding vertex and by its degree with respect to other sets. We also calculate quantum correlators in the general case of tripartite quantum graph states. We establish a relationship between these quantum properties and the structural properties of the corresponding tripartite graphs, including the number of non-overlapping neighbors, the number of common neighbors of the corresponding vertices, and the number of 4-cycles. As an illustrative example, we consider a tripartite graph forming a triangle and compute the entanglement distance using quantum simulations on the AerSimulator with noise models. The numerical results are consistent with the theoretical predictions. The obtained results demonstrate that quantum graph states provide an effective framework for studying structural properties of tripartite graphs. They open up the possibility of investigating such properties using quantum programming. It is worth highlighting that tripartite graphs have applications in solving practical problems such as resource allocation, scheduling, and database and hypergraph modeling.
summary: This paper develops a framework to map the structural properties of tripartite graphs onto the entanglement and correlation properties of multi-qubit states. It demonstrates that quantum programming can be used to study classical graph theory features like cycles and neighbor overlaps. The theoretical predictions are successfully validated using noisy quantum simulations.
main_problem: The paper aims to establish a quantitative relationship between the structural properties of weighted tripartite graphs and the quantum properties (entanglement and correlators) of multi-qubit quantum graph states.
main_result: The authors derive analytical expressions linking quantum correlators to graph features like 4-cycles and common neighbors, and demonstrate that qubit entanglement depends on vertex degrees and edge weights.
method: The study uses analytical derivations for entanglement distance and Pauli expectation values, complemented by quantum simulations on IBM AerSimulator with noise models to validate the theory.
model_or_system: The system consists of multi-qubit quantum graph states representing weighted and directed tripartite graphs, constructed by applying rotation gates (Rxy, Rxz, Ryz) to an initial separable state.
key_observables: Entanglement distance (EED), quantum correlators (Pauli expectation values), vertex degrees, and graph metrics like 4-cycles and common neighbors.
important_parameters: Gate rotation angles (phi, theta, alpha), edge weights, and vertex set partitions (U, V, W).
main_assumptions: The graph is assumed to be tripartite (no edges within the same set), and specific cases assume unweighted graphs with uniform initial parameters.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the quantum circuit protocol; Figures 2, 4, and 6 compare analytical entanglement distance surfaces with noisy simulation data; Figures 3, 5, and 7 show the absolute error between analytical and simulated results.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the construction of tripartite quantum graph states, provides analytical derivations for entanglement and correlators, establishes the link to graph topology, and concludes with numerical validation via noisy quantum simulation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: Entanglement distance of qubit q[0] with other qubits in state (51) for α = 0 and different values of θ, ϕ. The results obtained using the AerSimulator which includes a readout error of the order 10−2, a Pauli-X error of 10−4, and a CNOT error of 10−2 are indicated by cross markers, while the continuous surface represents the corresponding analytical calculations.

Figure 3: Absolute differences d0 between the analytical results for the entanglement distance of qubit q[0] with other qubits in state (51) for α = 0 and different values of θ, ϕ. and results obtained using the AerSimulator which includes a readout error of the order 10−2, a Pauli-X error of 10−4, and a CNOT error of 10−2.
